preFix.title: Re-covering a drop-in seat pad
preFix.subtitle: Strip and re-cover a drop-in chair seat pad with new fabric over the existing or replaced foam
preFix.excerpt: How to re-cover a drop-in chair seat pad with new upholstery fabric, covering stripping the old cover and any worn wadding, positioning and pulling the new fabric square, stapling from the centre outward, and pleating the corners neatly.
preFix.sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Cassell's Household Guide (upholstery). Dix and Miller Modern Upholstery (1948).

content.text: Re-covering a drop-in seat pad is a beginner upholstery project that makes a visible difference to a tired dining chair. The key technique is the
method: pulling the fabric from opposite sides before stapling keeps the top face smooth and the weave aligned. The
at each corner gives a clean, professional finish.

Upholstery fabric: cut a piece 150 mm larger than the seat pad in each direction (this allows 75 mm of turn-under on all four sides). A woven fabric with some stiffness (boucle, linen, velvet) is easier to pleat at corners than a stretchy jersey or very loose weave. Dacron polyester wadding to wrap over the foam if the pad surface is uneven. Replacement foam if the original is compressed flat: CMHR foam at 50 mm thick, medium density, cut to the foam footprint.

Flip the seat pad upside down. Use the staple remover to lift each staple from the underside of the frame, then peel the old fabric away. Check the foam: if it is still firm and at least 40 mm thick, it can be reused. If it is compressed, crumbling, or discoloured, cut a replacement to the same footprint. Old horsehair or cotton stuffing under the fabric can be replaced with a CMHR foam block of the same thickness.

Lay the new fabric face down on the bench. Place the seat pad face down on top of it, centred. Fold one side of the fabric up over the frame edge and place a single temporary staple at the centre of that side into the underside of the frame. Pull the opposite side taut and place a single temporary staple at its centre. Repeat for the other two sides. The fabric should now be held centred and evenly tensioned on all four sides.
Step 3, staple from the centre outward

Drive permanent staples outward from the centre of each side, working toward the corners in turn. Pull the fabric firmly before each staple. Alternate between opposite sides to keep tension even. Stop each run about 50 mm from the corners to leave room for the corner pleat. Check the top face of the pad after stapling each side: the weave should be straight and the surface wrinkle-free.

At each corner, fold the fabric point in first, then fold one side of the excess fabric back over the corner edge in a single pleat so the fold aligns with the corner of the frame. Secure the pleat with a staple across the fold. Trim away any excess fabric from the underside with the shears, leaving 20 mm of turn-under. Repeat at all four corners. Use the upholstery regulator through the fabric to push any wrinkles in the top face toward the edge before stapling the final runs.

For a patterned fabric, position the pattern centrally on the seat before placing the temporary staples in step 2. A bold motif should sit in the centre of the top face. Velvet must be applied in one pile direction: the pile should run from back to front of the seat. Wrap a layer of Dacron over the foam before covering to round off the edges of the foam block and give the finished pad a softer, more rounded look.

Spot-clean upholstery fabric with a damp cloth and a small amount of upholstery cleaner. Do not soak the fabric: excess water can stain through to the foam and encourage mould in the padding. Re-cover the seats every 10 to 15 years when the fabric begins to wear thin at the front edge of each seat.

attrs.fix: Remove the staples from the tightest side and re-pull the fabric from the loose side first before re-stapling. Work from opposite sides in small steps rather than completing one side before starting the next.
attrs.cause: The fabric was tensioned unevenly: one side was pulled harder than the opposite side, distorting the weave.
attrs.symptom: Fabric is wrinkled on the top face after the sides are fully stapled

attrs.fix: Remove the corner staple, trim away the excess fabric from the pleat before re-folding, and re-staple. The pleat should be a single layer of fabric folded over itself, not a full tuck of excess.
attrs.cause: Too much fabric was folded into the pleat, leaving a double thickness under the fold that shows on the top.
attrs.symptom: Corner pleat has a visible ridge on the top face of the seat
